note_data.title: Chemical Reactivity
keyPoints: Chemical reactivity is the tendency of an element to take part in chemical reactions by losing, gaining or sharing electrons
Alkali metals (Group IA) are most reactive metals due to single valence electron which is easily lost
Halogens (Group VIIA) are most reactive non-metals due to seven valence electrons, need only one electron to complete octet
Reactivity of metals increases down the group and decreases across the period from left to right
Reactivity of non-metals decreases down the group and increases across the period from left to right
Noble gases (Group VIIIA) are least reactive due to complete outer electron shell
Activity series arranges metals in order of decreasing reactivity: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au
More reactive metals displace less reactive metals from their salt solutions

definitions.definition: Chemical reactivity is the tendency of an element to take part in chemical reactions by losing, gaining or sharing electrons to complete its outermost shell.
Activity series is an arrangement of metals in order of their decreasing reactivity.
A displacement reaction is a chemical reaction in which a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound.
Noble gases are elements of Group VIIIA which have complete outermost electron shell and are chemically inert or least reactive.

importantQuestions.question: Why are alkali metals most reactive?
importantQuestions.answer: Alkali metals are most reactive because they have only one electron in their outermost shell which can be easily lost to achieve stable electronic configuration of nearest noble gas.

importantQuestions.question: Explain the trend of reactivity in periods and groups of periodic table with examples.
importantQuestions.answer: In groups: Reactivity of metals increases down the group because atomic size increases, valence electrons are farther from nucleus and easily removed. Example: Li < Na < K. Reactivity of non-metals decreases down the group because atomic size increases, attraction for additional electrons decreases. Example: F > Cl > Br. In periods: Reactivity of metals decreases across period because nuclear charge increases, electrons held more tightly. Reactivity of non-metals increases across period because nuclear charge increases, attraction for electrons increases. Example: Na > Mg > Al and N < O < F.

mcqs.question: Which metal is most reactive among the following?
Which group of elements is least reactive?
In displacement reaction Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu, zinc displaces copper because:

options.A: Zinc is less reactive than copper
options.B: Zinc is more reactive than copper

commonMistakes: Students often confuse reactivity trends - remember metals become MORE reactive down the group, non-metals become LESS reactive
Don't mix up activity series order - learn the sequence K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au
In displacement reactions, always remember more reactive displaces less reactive, not the opposite
Students forget that noble gases are inert due to complete outer shell, not due to single or seven electrons
Don't confuse Group numbers with electron configuration - Group IA has 1 valence electron, not atomic number 1
examTips: Activity series question appears in every BSEK paper - memorize the complete sequence
Reactivity trends usually asked as 5-8 mark long question - explain both period and group trends
Displacement reaction examples frequently asked - practice with Pakistani context like copper extraction
Definition of chemical reactivity is commonly asked - learn exact textbook wording
Draw electronic configuration diagrams to explain reactivity trends - this scores extra marks
note_data.keyDiagram: Periodic table showing reactivity trends with arrows - metals increasing reactivity down groups and decreasing across periods, non-metals showing opposite trends. Include Group IA (most reactive metals), Group VIIA (most reactive non-metals), and Group VIIIA (least reactive/inert).
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — asked students to define chemical reactivity and explain factors affecting it
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q7 (6 marks) — required comparison of reactivity of metals with examples from activity series
BSEK 2024 SSC-II Q4 (5 marks) — asked to define activation energy and explain its role in chemical reactions
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q6 (3 marks) — tested knowledge of factors that increase rate of chemical reactions

workedExample.question: Define chemical reactivity. Explain any three factors that affect the rate of chemical reactions. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Write the textbook definition of chemical reactivity
Step 2: List three factors affecting reaction rate
Step 3: Explain each factor with proper reasoning
workedExample.answer: Chemical reactivity is the tendency of a substance to undergo chemical change by losing, gaining or sharing electrons with other substances. Three factors affecting rate of chemical reactions are: (1) Temperature - Higher temperature increases kinetic energy of molecules, leading to more frequent collisions and faster reactions. (2) Concentration - Higher concentration of reactants increases collision frequency, thus increasing reaction rate. (3) Surface area - Smaller particle size provides greater surface area for reaction, increasing the rate of reaction.
workedExample.markScheme: Definition of chemical reactivity (2 marks), naming three factors (1 mark), correct explanation of each factor (1 mark each)

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for chemical reactivity definition.
Many students confuse 'rate of reaction' with 'chemical reactivity' - ensure you understand both concepts separately as per textbook definitions.
When explaining factors affecting reaction rate, provide complete explanations with proper scientific reasoning, not just listing factors.
note_data.quickRecap: Chemical reactivity is the tendency to undergo chemical change by electron transfer. Main factors affecting reaction rate include temperature, concentration, surface area, and presence of catalysts. Metals show different reactivity levels as shown in activity series. Higher temperature and concentration always increase reaction rate due to increased collision frequency.
